1.o: Isaiah and Malachi each spake concerning the beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, in the ministry of John. From these prophets we may observe, that Christ, in his gospel, comes among us, bringing with him a treasure of grace, and a sceptre of government. Such is the corruption of the world, that there is great opposition to his progress. When God sent his Son into the world, he took care, and when he sends him into the heart, he takes care, to prepare his way before him. John thinks himself unworthy of the meanest office about Christ. The most eminent saints have always been the most humble. They feel their need of Christ's atoning blood and sanctifying Spirit, more than others. The great promise Christ makes in his gospel to those who have repented, and have had their sins forgiven them, is, they shall be baptized with the Holy Ghost; shall be purified by his graces, and refreshed by his comforts. We use the ordinances, word, and sacraments without profit and comfort, for the most part, because we have not of that Divine light within us; and we have it not because we ask it not; for we have his word that cannot fail, that our heavenly Father will give this light, his Holy Spirit, to those that ask it.
1.m:
Isaiah and Malachi both talked about the start of Jesus' message through John the Baptist. These prophets show us that when Christ brings his message, he comes with gifts of kindness and power to rule. 

The world is full of sin, so people often resist his teachings. When God sent Jesus to Earth, he made sure to prepare the way ahead of time. And when God sends Jesus to someone’s heart, he prepares the person first.

John felt he wasn’t good enough even for the lowest job around Jesus. Great leaders have always been very humble. They know they need Jesus’ forgiveness and help more than anyone else.

Jesus promises in his message that if you turn away from sin and get forgiveness, you’ll receive the Holy Spirit. This means you'll be cleaned by God’s goodness and comforted by his peace. But we don't usually get much out of church services or readings unless we have God’s light inside us. And we don’t have that light because we don’t ask for it. Yet God has promised to give us his Holy Spirit if we ask.

1.o: Christ's baptism was his first public appearance, after he had long lived unknown. How much hidden worth is there, which in this world is not known! But sooner or later it shall be known, as Christ was. He took upon himself the likeness of sinful flesh; and thus, for our sakes, he sanctified himself, that we also might be sanctified, and be baptized with him, Joh 17:19. See how honourably God owned him, when he submitted to John's baptism. He saw the Spirit descending upon him like a dove. We may see heaven opened to us, when we perceive the Spirit descending and working upon us. God's good work in us, is sure evidence of his good will towards us, and preparations for us. As to Christ's temptation, Mark notices his being in the wilderness and that he was with the wild beasts. It was an instance of his Father's care of him, which encouraged him the more that his Father would provide for him. Special protections are earnests of seasonable supplies. The serpent tempted the first Adam in the garden, the Second Adam in the wilderness; with different success indeed; and ever since he still tempts the children of both, in all places and conditions. Company and conversation have their temptations; and being alone, even in a wilderness, has its own also. No place or state exempts, no business, not lawful labouring, eating, or drinking, not even fasting and praying; often in these duties there are the most assaults, but in them is the sweetest victory. The ministration of the good angels is matter of great comfort in reference to the malignant designs of the evil angels; but much more does it comfort us, to have the indwelling of God the Holy Spirit in our hearts.
1.m:
Christ’s baptism marked his first public appearance after living quietly for a long time. There is so much unseen value in the world that goes unnoticed, but eventually, it will come to light, just as Christ did. By taking on human form, he made himself holy for us, so we could become holy too, as mentioned in John 17:19. Notice how God honored Jesus by having him baptized by John. When Jesus was baptized, he saw the Holy Spirit coming down on him like a dove. This shows us that God approves of what happens in our lives when we feel the Spirit moving within us. God’s work in us proves his love and preparation for us.

Mark mentions that Jesus went into the wilderness and was with wild animals during his testing. His Father protected him, which gave him confidence that help would always be available. Knowing God cares for you encourages you through tough times. Angels watched over Jesus during his trials, offering comfort against Satan’s attacks. Just as angels helped Jesus, knowing the Holy Spirit lives inside us brings even greater peace and strength. 

Satan tested the first Adam in the Garden and tried to test Jesus in the desert, but Jesus won. Since then, Satan keeps trying to tempt everyone, whether in crowds or alone. Temptation comes everywhere—during work, meals, prayer, or fasting. Often, the greatest victories happen while doing those things. Having angels around helps protect us from evil spirits, but feeling the Holy Spirit within gives us even more comfort and power.

1.o: Jesus began to preach in Galilee, after that John was put in prison. If some be laid aside, others shall be raised up, to carry on the same work. Observe the great truths Christ preached. By repentance we give glory to our Creator whom we have offended; by faith we give glory to our Redeemer who came to save us from our sins. Christ has joined these two together, and let no man think to put them asunder. Christ puts honour upon those who, though mean in this world, are diligent in their business and kind to one another. Industry and unity are good and pleasant, and the Lord Jesus commands a blessing on them. Those whom Christ calls, must leave all to follow him; and by his grace he makes them willing to do so. Not that we must needs go out of the world, but we must sit loose to the world; forsake every thing that is against our duty to Christ, and that cannot be kept without hurt to our souls. Jesus strictly kept the sabbath day, by applying himself unto, and abounding in the sabbath work, in order to which the sabbath rest was appointed. There is much in the doctrine of Christ that is astonishing; and the more we hear it, the more cause we see to admire it.
1.m:
Jesus started preaching in Galilee after John was put in jail. When one person stops doing God’s work, someone else will take over.

Notice what Jesus taught. Repentance means saying sorry for the wrong things we’ve done to God. Faith means believing in Jesus, who came to save us from sin. Jesus brought these ideas together, and we shouldn’t separate them.

Jesus honored people who worked hard even if they didn't have much money. He blessed those who were industrious and got along well with others. 

When Jesus calls someone, they need to leave everything behind to follow him. With God’s help, people become happy to do this. We don’t have to leave the world, but we should stop holding onto anything that goes against following Jesus.

Jesus always followed the rules about keeping the Sabbath day holy. He used the time to focus on worshiping God and helping others.

There’s a lot in Jesus’ teachings that surprises us. The more we listen, the more amazing we find it all to be.

1.o: The devil is an unclean spirit, because he has lost all the purity of his nature, because he acts in direct opposition to the Holy Spirit of God, and by his suggestions defiles the spirits of men. There are many in our assemblies who quietly attend under merely formal teachers; but if the Lord come with faithful ministers and holy doctrine, and by his convincing Spirit, they are ready to say, like this man, What have we to do with thee, Jesus of Nazareth! No disorder could enable a man to know Jesus to be the Holy One of God. He desires to have nothing to do with Jesus, for he despairs of being saved by him, and dreads being destroyed by him. See whose language those speak, that say to the Almighty, Depart from us. This unclean spirit hated and dreaded Christ, because he knew him to be a Holy One; for the carnal mind is enmity against God, especially against his holiness. When Christ by his grace delivers souls out of the hands of Satan, it is not without tumult in the soul; for that spiteful enemy will disquiet those whom he cannot destroy. This put all who saw it upon considering, What is this new doctrine? A work as great often is wrought now, yet men treat it with contempt and neglect. If this were not so, the conversion of a notorious wicked man to a sober, righteous, and godly life, by the preaching of a crucified Saviour, would cause many to ask, What doctrine is this?
1.m:
The devil is an unclean spirit because he has lost all his purity. He works against the Holy Spirit of God and corrupts people’s minds through his evil ideas.

There are people in our churches who only go along with empty teachings. But when true ministers and good doctrines come, some might reject them, just like this person did. They said, “What do you want with me, Jesus?”

No one in their right mind would refuse to recognize Jesus as the Holy One of God. This man wanted nothing to do with Jesus because he thought he couldn’t be saved by him and feared being judged by him.

People who tell God to stay away show what they really think. The unclean spirit was afraid of Christ because he knew Christ was holy. People who don't love God hate his holiness.

When Christ saves someone from Satan, there is turmoil inside them. Even though Satan can’t destroy them, he still tries to upset them.

This made everyone wonder about these new teachings. Sometimes amazing things happen today too, but people ignore them. If they didn’t, seeing a bad person become good after hearing about Jesus dying on the cross would make others curious. They’d ask, “What kind of teaching is this?”

1.o: Wherever Christ comes, he comes to do good. He cures, that we may minister to him, and to others who are his, and for his sake. Those kept from public ordinances by sickness or other real hinderances, may expect the Saviour's gracious presence; he will soothe their sorrows, and abate their pains. Observe how numerous the patients were. When others speed well with Christ, it should quicken us in seeking after him. Christ departed into a solitary place. Though he was in no danger of distraction, or of temptation to vain-glory, yet he retired. Those who have the most business in public, and of the best kind, must yet sometimes be alone with God.
1.m:
Whenever Jesus visits somewhere, he comes to help people. He heals them so that we can serve him and those who belong to him. People who can't go to church because of illness or other reasons can still receive the Savior’s care. He will comfort them and ease their pain.

Notice how many sick people there were. When others get better after meeting Jesus, it should encourage us to seek him too.

Jesus went to a quiet place. Even though he wasn’t at risk of becoming distracted or proud, he needed time alone. People who are very busy helping others need times to be alone with God too.

1.o: We have here Christ's cleansing of a leper. It teaches us to apply to the Saviour with great humility, and with full submission to his will, saying, “Lord, if thou wilt,” without any doubt of Christ's readiness to help the distressed. See also what to expect from Christ; that according to our faith it shall be to us. The poor leper said, If thou wilt. Christ readily wills favours to those who readily refer themselves to his will. Christ would have nothing done that looked like seeking praise of the people. But no reasons now exist why we should hesitate to spread the praises of Christ.
1.m:
Here we see Jesus healing a leper. This shows us to ask for help from the Savior humbly, saying, "If you want to." We shouldn't doubt that he wants to help those in need. 

The sick man asked, "If you wish." And Jesus was happy to grant favors to those who trust him fully. 

Jesus did not do anything to get praise from people. Now there is no reason for us to hold back from praising him.

2.o: It was this man's misery that he needed to be so carried, and shows the suffering state of human life; it was kind of those who so carried him, and teaches the compassion that should be in men, toward their fellow-creatures in distress. True faith and strong faith may work in various ways; but it shall be accepted and approved by Jesus Christ. Sin is the cause of all our pains and sicknesses. The way to remove the effect, is to take away the cause. Pardon of sin strikes at the root of all diseases. Christ proved his power to forgive sin, by showing his power to cure the man sick of the palsy. And his curing diseases was a figure of his pardoning sin, for sin is the disease of the soul; when it is pardoned, it is healed. When we see what Christ does in healing souls, we must own that we never saw the like. Most men think themselves whole; they feel no need of a physician, therefore despise or neglect Christ and his gospel. But the convinced, humbled sinner, who despairs of all help, excepting from the Saviour, will show his faith by applying to him without delay.
2.m:
This man’s situation showed how hard life can be. People helping him were kind, which teaches us to have compassion on others who are struggling.

True faith can show itself in different ways, but Jesus accepts and approves of it. Our sins cause all our pain and illnesses. To get rid of these problems, we need to remove the cause—our sins. Forgiving sins gets rid of the root of all our troubles. 

Jesus showed he could forgive sins by healing the paralyzed man. Healing people was like forgiving their sins because sin is an illness of the soul. When sins are forgiven, the soul is healed. 

When we see what Christ does in healing souls, we must admit we’ve never seen anything like it before. Many people think they’re fine and don’t need a doctor, so they ignore or dismiss Christ and his message. But those who know they need help from the Savior will quickly turn to him.

2.o: Matthew was not a good character, or else, being a Jew, he would never have been a publican, that is, a tax-gatherer for the Romans. However, Christ called this publican to follow him. With God, through Christ, there is mercy to pardon the greatest sins, and grace to change the greatest sinners, and make them holy. A faithful, fair-dealing publican was rare. And because the Jews had a particular hatred to an office which proved that they were subject to the Romans, they gave these tax-gatherers an ill name. But such as these our blessed Lord did not hesitate to converse with, when he appeared in the likeness of sinful flesh. And it is no new thing for that which is both well done and well designed, to be slandered, and turned to the reproach of the wisest and best of men. Christ would not withdraw, though the Pharisees were offended. If the world had been righteous, there had been no occasion for his coming, either to preach repentance, or to purchase forgiveness. We must not keep company with ungodly men out of love to their vain conversation; but we are to show love to their souls, remembering that our good Physician had the power of healing in himself, and was in no danger of taking the disease; but it is not so with us. In trying to do good to others, let us be careful we do not get harm to ourselves.
2.m:
Matthew was not a good person. As a Jew, he would not have worked as a tax collector for the Romans if he had been. Still, Jesus called Matthew to follow him. Through God’s mercy and grace, even great sinners can become holy. Honest tax collectors were uncommon. The Jews hated the tax-collecting job because it showed they were under Roman rule. So, they gave tax collectors bad reputations. Yet, Jesus didn’t avoid talking to people like Matthew. 

Sometimes, good actions by wise people are criticized. When the Pharisees got upset, Jesus did not stop helping those who needed it. If the world had been just, Jesus wouldn't have needed to come to teach about changing hearts or forgive sins. We shouldn’t hang around with wicked people just to enjoy their talk. Instead, we should care about saving their souls. Remember, Jesus could heal without getting sick. But we might hurt ourselves while trying to help others.

2.o: Strict professors are apt to blame all that do not fully come up to their own views. Christ did not escape slanders; we should be willing to bear them, as well as careful not to deserve them; but should attend to every part of our duty in its proper order and season.
2.m: People who set high standards tend to criticize those who don't meet their expectations. Even Jesus faced criticism. We should be ready to handle insults if we haven’t done anything wrong. But we should also focus on doing our duties at the right time and in the right way.

2.o: The sabbath is a sacred and Divine institution; a privilege and benefit, not a task and drudgery. God never designed it to be a burden to us, therefore we must not make it so to ourselves. The sabbath was instituted for the good of mankind, as living in society, having many wants and troubles, preparing for a state of happiness or misery. Man was not made for the sabbath, as if his keeping it could be of service to God, nor was he commanded to keep it outward observances to his real hurt. Every observance respecting it, is to be interpreted by the rule of mercy.
2.m:
The Sabbath is a special day given by God. It’s meant to help people, not harm them. God didn’t intend for it to be a burden. 

He created the Sabbath to benefit everyone living together, dealing with all kinds of needs and problems. People were not made just to keep the Sabbath, as if doing so helps God. Instead, God told us to follow the rules of the Sabbath without hurting our true well-being.

We should always remember to treat others kindly while observing the Sabbath.

3.o: This man's case was piteous; he had a withered hand, which disabled him from working for his living; and those that are so, are the most proper objects of charity. Let those be helped that cannot help themselves. But stubborn infidels, when they can say nothing against the truth, yet will not yield. We hear what is said amiss, and see what is done amiss; but Christ looks at the root of bitterness in the heart, the blindness and hardness of that, and is grieved. Let hard-hearted sinners tremble to think of the anger with which he will look upon them shortly, when the day of his wrath comes. The great healing day now is the sabbath, and the healing place the house of prayer; but the healing power is of Christ. The gospel command is like that recorded here: though our hands are withered, yet, if we will not stretch them out, it is our own fault that we are not healed. But if we are healed, Christ, his power and grace, must have all the glory.
3.m:
This man’s situation was sad; he had a withered hand that kept him from working. People who can’t work need extra help. 

We should assist those who can't help themselves. But some people refuse to accept the truth even when they can't argue against it. They still find things wrong with what others say and do. 

Christ sees past the surface. He notices the real problems inside us—our stubbornness and lack of understanding. This makes him feel sorry. Sinful people with hard hearts should worry about God’s anger on judgment day. 

Today, Jesus heals during worship services on the Sabbath. His power brings healing. Just like in the story, if someone doesn’t reach out their hand to be healed, they miss out because of their own choice. But if they do reach out, then Jesus gets all the credit for healing them.

3.o: All our sicknesses and calamities spring from the anger of God against our sins. Their removal, or the making them blessings to us, was purchased to us by the blood of Christ. But the plagues and diseases of our souls, of our hearts, are chiefly to be dreaded; and He can heal them also by a word. May more and more press to Christ to be healed of these plagues, and to be delivered from the enemies of their souls.
3.m: All our illnesses and troubles come from God’s anger because of our sins. The cure for them, turning them into blessings, came through the death of Christ. But the real dangers are the spiritual problems in our hearts. Jesus can heal those too with just His words. Let more people turn to Christ to be healed of these spiritual troubles and set free from what harms their souls.

3.o: Christ calls whom he will; for his grace is his own. He had called the apostles to separate themselves from the crowd, and they came unto him. He now gave them power to heal sicknesses, and to cast out devils. May the Lord send forth more and more of those who have been with him, and have learned of him to preach his gospel, to be instruments in his blessed work. Those whose hearts are enlarged in the work of God, can easily bear with what is inconvenient to themselves, and will rather lose a meal than an opportunity of doing good. Those who go on with zeal in the work of God, must expect hinderances, both from the hatred of enemies, and mistaken affections of friends, and need to guard against both.
3.m: Jesus chooses whoever he wants because his grace is his own. Before, he called the apostles away from the crowds, and they followed him. Now, he gives them the power to heal sick people and drive out demons. The Lord sends out more people who have been with Jesus and learned from him to spread the gospel. People who are generous about God’s work can handle things that are uncomfortable for them. They would rather miss a meal than miss a chance to help others. When you work hard for God, you might face problems. Enemies may hate you, and even well-meaning friends might get in your way. You need to watch out for both kinds of trouble.

3.o: It was plain that the doctrine of Christ had a direct tendency to break the devil's power; and it was as plain, that casting of him out of the bodies of people, confirmed that doctrine; therefore Satan could not support such a design. Christ gave an awful warning against speaking such dangerous words. It is true the gospel promises, because Christ has purchased, forgiveness for the greatest sins and sinners; but by this sin, they would oppose the gifts of the Holy Ghost after Christ's ascension. Such is the enmity of the heart, that unconverted men pretend believers are doing Satan's work, when sinners are brought to repentance and newness of life.
3.m:
It was clear that Jesus' teachings weakened the devil’s power. And when he drove evil spirits out of people, it proved his message was true. So the devil couldn’t help those who spoke against Jesus.

Jesus warned them not to speak such harmful things. The good news teaches that Jesus can forgive even the worst sins. But saying these bad words goes against what God wants. 

Unchanged hearts often claim that turning sinners into believers helps the devil. They think bringing people to repentance and new life is actually helping Satan.

3.o: It is a great comfort to all true Christians, that they are dearer to Christ than mother, brother, or sister as such, merely as relations in the flesh would have been, even had they been holy. Blessed be God, this great and gracious privilege is ours even now; for though Christ's bodily presence cannot be enjoyed by us, his spiritual presence is not denied us.
3.m: True Christians can feel happy knowing that Jesus loves them more than he would love a mother, brother, or sister just because of family ties. This wonderful gift is ours right now. Even if we can't see Jesus face-to-face, he is still spiritually close to us.

4.o: This parable contained instruction so important, that all capable of hearing were bound to attend to it. There are many things we are concerned to know; and if we understand not the plain truths of the gospel, how shall we learn those more difficult! It will help us to value the privileges we enjoy as disciples of Christ, if we seriously consider the deplorable state of all who have not such privileges. In the great field of the church, the word of God is dispensed to all. Of the many that hear the word of the gospel, but few receive it, so as to bring forth fruit. Many are much affected with the word for the present, who yet receive no abiding benefit. The word does not leave abiding impressions upon the minds of men, because their hearts are not duly disposed to receive it. The devil is very busy about careless hearers, as the fowls of the air go about the seed that lies above ground. Many continue in a barren, false profession, and go down to hell. Impressions that are not deep, will not last. Many do not mind heart-work, without which religion is nothing. Others are hindered from profiting by the word of God, by abundance of the world. And those who have but little of the world, may yet be ruined by indulging the body. God expects and requires fruit from those who enjoy the gospel, a temper of mind and Christian graces daily exercised, Christian duties duly performed. Let us look to the Lord, that by his new-creating grace our hearts may become good ground, and that the good seed of the word may produce in our lives those good words and works which are through Jesus Christ, to the praise and glory of God the Father.
4.m:
This story teaches an important lesson that everyone should listen to carefully. We need to understand basic truths before we can grasp harder ones. Thinking about how lucky we are to have the teachings of Christ helps us appreciate them more. 

In the big family of believers, God’s message reaches everyone. But out of many people who hear the gospel, only a few really accept it and grow spiritually. Some feel moved by the message at first, but don’t get lasting benefits. The word doesn't stick in people's minds unless their hearts are ready to receive it.

The devil tries hard to stop people from truly understanding the message, just like birds eat seeds left on top of the soil. Some pretend to follow Christ but end up going to hell. Shallow impressions won’t last. People often neglect the work of the heart, making their faith meaningless.

Others are stopped from growing by having too much stuff. Even those with less material wealth can still ruin themselves by pampering their bodies. God wants us to show the fruits of our faith—good attitudes and actions—in our daily lives. We should ask the Lord to shape our hearts so that the truth we hear leads to real growth and goodness.

4.o: These declarations were intended to call the attention of the disciples to the word of Christ. By his thus instructing them, they were made able to instruct others; as candles are lighted, not to be covered, but to be placed on a candlestick, that they may give light to a room. This parable of the good seed, shows the manner in which the kingdom of God makes progress in the world. Let but the word of Christ have the place it ought to have in a soul, and it will show itself in a good conversation. It grows gradually: first the blade; then the ear; after that the full corn in the ear. When it is sprung up, it will go forward. The work of grace in the soul is, at first, but the day of small things; yet it has mighty products even now, while it is in its growth; but what will there be when it is perfected in heaven!
4.m:
These statements were meant to get the disciples' attention about Jesus' teachings. By teaching them, he gave them the ability to teach others too—like lighting candles to put on a stand so they could shine brightly in a room. 

This story about the good seeds shows how the kingdom of God spreads through the world. If people let Christ’s words take their proper place in their hearts, those words will lead to good behavior. 

The process happens slowly: First comes the sprout, then the stalk appears, followed by the full head of grain. Once it starts growing, it keeps going. 

When God works in someone’s life, it begins with small steps. But even during this time of growth, there are great results. Imagine how wonderful it will be when that person reaches perfection in heaven!

4.o: Christ was asleep in the storm, to try the faith of his disciples, and to stir them up to pray. Their faith appeared weak, and their prayers strong. When our wicked hearts are like the troubled sea which cannot rest, when our passions are unruly, let us think we hear the law of Christ, saying, Be silent, be dumb. When without are fightings, and within are fears, and the spirits are in a tumult, if he say, “Peace, be still,” there is a great calm at once. Why are ye so fearful? Though there may be cause for some fear, yet not for such fear as this. Those may suspect their faith, who can have such a thought as that Jesus careth not though his people perish. How imperfect are the best of saints! Faith and fear take their turns while we are in this world; but ere long, fear will be overcome, and faith will be lost in sight.
4.m:
Christ was sleeping during the storm to test the faith of his followers and encourage them to pray. Their faith seemed weak, but their prayers were strong. 

When our hearts are restless like a troubled sea, and our emotions run wild, we should remember what Christ said: Be quiet, be still. 

Even when we face battles outside and worries inside, making our minds chaotic, if he tells us, "Be calm," everything becomes peaceful right away.

Why are you so afraid? Even if there’s reason to be scared, it shouldn’t be this kind of fear.

We might doubt our faith if we think Jesus doesn't care whether his people suffer. 

The best believers still struggle sometimes. Faith and fear come and go while we’re here on earth. But one day, fear will disappear, and faith will turn into seeing God clearly.

5.o: Some openly wilful sinners are like this madman. The commands of the law are as chains and fetters, to restrain sinners from their wicked courses; but they break those bands in sunder; and it is an evidence of the power of the devil in them. A legion of soldiers consisted of six thousand men, or more. What multitudes of fallen spirits there must be, and all enemies to God and man, when here was a legion in one poor wretched creature! Many there are that rise up against us. We are not a match for our spiritual enemies, in our own strength; but in the Lord, and in the power of his might, we shall be able to stand against them, though there are legions of them. When the vilest transgressor is delivered by the power of Jesus from the bondage of Satan, he will gladly sit at the feet of his Deliverer, and hear his word, who delivers the wretched slaves of Satan, and numbers them among his saints and servants. When the people found that their swine were lost, they had a dislike to Christ. Long-suffering and mercy may be seen, even in the corrections by which men lose their property while their lives are saved, and warning given them to seek the salvation of their souls. The man joyfully proclaimed what great things Jesus had done for him. All men marvelled, but few followed him. Many who cannot but wonder at the works of Christ, yet do not, as they ought, wonder after him.
5.m:
Some openly wilful sinners act like this madman. The laws try to stop them from doing bad things, but they break free. This shows how strong the devil is inside them. A legion has about six thousand soldiers. There must be many evil spirits if one person has a whole legion. 

Many enemies attack us spiritually. On our own, we can't fight them. But with God’s help, we can win, even if there are thousands of them. When someone very sinful is freed from Satan by Jesus, they thank Jesus and follow his teachings. They become part of God’s family.

When the people saw their pigs were gone, they didn’t like Jesus. Sometimes, losing possessions teaches us important lessons. It helps us save our souls. 

The man happily told everyone how Jesus helped him. People were amazed, but only a few started following Jesus. Many wonder at Jesus’ miracles but don’t truly believe and follow him.

5.o: A despised gospel will go where it will be better received. One of the rulers of a synagogue earnestly besought Christ for a little daughter, about twelve years old, who was dying. Another cure was wrought by the way. We should do good, not only when in the house, but when we walk by the way, De 6:7. It is common with people not to apply to Christ till they have tried in vain all other helpers, and find them, as certainly they will, physicians of no value. Some run to diversions and gay company; others plunge into business, or even into intemperance; others go about to establish their own righteousness, or torment themselves by vain superstitions. Many perish in these ways; but none will ever find rest to the soul by such devices; while those whom Christ heals of the disease of sin, find in themselves an entire change for the better. As secret acts of sin, so secret acts of faith, are known to the Lord Jesus. The woman told all the truth. It is the will of Christ that his people should be comforted, and he has power to command comfort to troubled spirits. The more simply we depend on Him, and expect great things from him, the more we shall find in ourselves that he is become our salvation. Those who, by faith, are healed of their spiritual diseases, have reason to go in peace.
5.m: A rejected message will travel to places where it will be better accepted. A leader at a synagogue asked Christ to help his little girl, who was about twelve years old and very sick. Along the way, another healing took place. We should do good deeds not just when we're at home, but wherever we go, as Deuteronomy 6:7 teaches. People often wait until other methods fail before turning to Christ. They might try distractions, fun activities, or busy work. Others may turn to alcohol or strict religious practices. But none of these bring true peace. Only those healed by Christ see real improvement. Even private sins and private acts of faith are known to Jesus. This woman shared everything honestly. Jesus wants his followers to feel comforted, and he commands troubled souls to find peace. The more we trust him and believe in his power, the more we'll experience his saving grace. Those who are healed spiritually through faith have reason to live peacefully.

5.o: We may suppose Jairus hesitating whether he should ask Christ to go on or not, when told that his daughter was dead. But have we not as much occasion for the grace of God, and the comfort of his Spirit, for the prayers of our ministers and Christian friends, when death is in the house, as when sickness is there? Faith is the only remedy against grief and fear at such a time. Believe the resurrection, then fear not. He raised the dead child to life by a word of power. Such is the gospel call to those who are by nature dead in trespasses and sins. It is by the word of Christ that spiritual life is given. All who saw it, and heard of it, admired the miracle, and Him that wrought it. Though we cannot now expect to have our dead children or relatives restored, we may hope to find comfort under our trials.
5.m:
We can imagine Jairus wondering if he should keep asking Jesus to come or stop when he learned his daughter had died. But don't we need God’s help just as much when someone dies as when they are sick?

Faith helps us deal with sadness and worry. Believe in the resurrection, and you won’t be afraid. Jesus brought the little girl back to life with his words. This shows how God calls people who were once spiritually dead because of their sins. Jesus gives them new life through his message.

Everyone who saw what happened and heard about it was amazed by the miracle and by Jesus himself. Even though we can’t bring back loved ones from the dead today, we can still find comfort during hard times.

6.o: Our Lord's countrymen tried to prejudice the minds of people against him. Is not this the carpenter? Our Lord Jesus probably had worked in that business with his father. He thus put honour upon mechanics, and encouraged all persons who eat by the labour of their hands. It becomes the followers of Christ to content themselves with the satisfaction of doing good, although they are denied the praise of it. How much did these Nazarenes lose by obstinate prejudices against Jesus! May Divine grace deliver us from that unbelief, which renders Christ a savour of death, rather than of life to the soul. Let us, like our Master, go and teach cottages and peasants the way of salvation.
6.m:
Our Lord's fellow townspeople tried to turn others against him. Isn't he the carpenter? Jesus likely worked at that trade with his dad. This shows he respected manual labor and encouraged everyone to work hard for a living. 

It suits those who follow Christ to be happy just knowing they're doing good, even if nobody praises them for it. These people from Nazareth missed out on so much because they were stubbornly against Jesus. We should ask God to free us from such disbelief, which makes Christ seem harmful instead of helpful.

Like his example, let’s go teach farmers and villagers about the path to salvation.

6.o: Though the apostles were conscious to themselves of great weakness, and expected no wordly advantage, yet, in obedience to their Master, and in dependence upon his strength, they went out. They did not amuse people with curious matters, but told them they must repent of their sins, and turn to God. The servants of Christ may hope to turn many from darkness unto God, and to heal souls by the power of the Holy Ghost.
6.m:
Even though the apostles knew they were weak and didn’t expect any worldly gain, they still obeyed their master and relied on his strength to go out. 

They didn’t entertain people with strange things. Instead, they told them to repent of their sins and turn to God. 

The followers of Christ can hope to lead many from darkness to God and heal souls through the power of the Holy Spirit.

6.o: Herod feared John while he lived, and feared him still more when he was dead. Herod did many of those things which John in his preaching taught him; but it is not enough to do many things, we must have respect to all the commandments. Herod respected John, till he touched him in his Herodias. Thus many love good preaching, if it keep far away from their beloved sin. But it is better that sinners persecute ministers now for faithfulness, than curse them eternally for unfaithfulness. The ways of God are unsearchable; but we may be sure he never can be at a loss to repay his servants for what they endure or lose for his sake. Death could not come so as to surprise this holy man; and the triumph of the wicked was short.
6.m:
Herod was afraid of John while he was alive, and even more after he died. Herod followed some of John’s teachings, but just doing some things isn’t enough. We need to follow all the commandments. 

Herod liked John until John spoke against Herod’s marriage to Herodias. Many people enjoy good messages unless they touch on their favorite sins. It’s better for sinners to attack faithful ministers now rather than punish them forever later. 

God always finds a way to reward his followers for what they suffer or lose because of him. Death couldn’t catch this holy man off guard, and the wicked don’t get to celebrate long.

6.o: Let not ministers do any thing or teach any thing, but what they are willing should be told to their Lord. Christ notices the frights of some, and the toils of others of his disciples, and provides rest for those that are tired, and refuge for those that are terrified. The people sought the spiritual food of Christ's word, and then he took care that they should not want bodily food. If Christ and his disciples put up with mean things, surely we may. And this miracle shows that Christ came into the world, not only to restore, but to preserve and nourish spiritual life; in him there is enough for all that come. None are sent empty away from Christ but those who come to him full of themselves. Though Christ had bread enough at command, he teaches us not to waste any of God's bounties, remembering how many are in want. We may, some time, need the fragments that we now throw away.
6.m: Ministers shouldn't do anything or teach anything that they wouldn't want their Lord to know about. Jesus noticed when some of his followers were scared and others were working hard, so he made sure to give rest to those who were tired and safety to those who were afraid. People wanted to hear the spiritual words of Jesus, and he made sure they didn’t go hungry either. If Jesus and his friends could live simply, we certainly can too. This miracle shows that Jesus came to help restore, protect, and feed our spiritual lives. With Jesus, there’s always enough for everyone who comes to him. Only those who think they don’t need anything leave without getting what they need from Jesus. Even though Jesus could have plenty of bread, he taught us not to waste God’s gifts, knowing that many are in need. Someday, we might find ourselves needing the leftovers we threw away today.

6.o: The church is often like a ship at sea, tossed with tempests, and not comforted: we may have Christ for us, yet wind and tide against us; but it is a comfort to Christ's disciples in a storm, that their Master is in the heavenly mount, interceding for them. And no difficulties can hinder Christ's appearance for his people, when the set time is come. He silenced their fears, by making himself known to them. Our fears are soon satisfied, if our mistakes are set right, especially our mistakes as to Christ. Let the disciples have their Master with them, and all is well. It is for want of rightly understanding Christ's former works, that we view his present works as if there never were the like before. If Christ's ministers now could cure people's bodily diseases, what multitudes would flock after them! It is sad to think how much more most care about their bodies than about their souls.
6.m: The church is often like a ship at sea, tossed by storms without comfort. We might have Christ on our side, yet face strong winds and currents against us. But it’s comforting to know that Jesus is in heaven praying for us. When the time comes, nothing can stop him from helping his followers. He calmed their fears by showing himself to them. Our worries fade quickly once we correct our misunderstandings, especially those about who Christ really is. As long as Jesus is with us, everything will be okay. People don’t see Christ’s work today because they forget his past miracles. If Christian leaders could heal sick bodies, many would follow them. Sadly, most people care more about their physical health than their spiritual wellbeing.

7.o: One great design of Christ's coming was, to set aside the ceremonial law; and to make way for this, he rejects the ceremonies men added to the law of God's making. Those clean hands and that pure heart which Christ bestows on his disciples, and requires of them, are very different from the outward and superstitious forms of Pharisees of every age. Jesus reproves them for rejecting the commandment of God. It is clear that it is the duty of children, if their parents are poor, to relieve them as far as they are able; and if children deserve to die that curse their parents, much more those that starve them. But if a man conformed to the traditions of the Pharisees, they found a device to free him from the claim of this duty.
7.m:
One main reason Jesus came was to do away with the old rules about rituals. To help with this, he refused the extra customs people added to God’s laws. The clean hands and pure hearts that Jesus gives his followers are very different from the fake religious acts of the Pharisees at all times.

Jesus tells them off for ignoring God’s commands. Clearly, kids should help support their parents if the parents need money. And just as cursing parents means death, not taking care of them deserves even worse punishment. Yet, if someone followed the Pharisees’ made-up rules, the leaders would find ways to excuse them from helping their parents.

7.o: Our wicked thoughts and affections, words and actions, defile us, and these only. As a corrupt fountain sends forth corrupt streams, so does a corrupt heart send forth corrupt reasonings, corrupt appetites and passions, and all the wicked words and actions that come from them. A spiritual understanding of the law of God, and a sense of the evil of sin, will cause a man to seek for the grace of the Holy Spirit, to keep down the evil thoughts and affections that work within.
7.m:
Our bad thoughts and feelings, our words and actions, are what make us unclean. Just like a dirty water source produces dirty water, an impure heart produces wrong ideas, bad desires, and angry feelings. These lead to sinful words and deeds.

Understanding God’s laws spiritually and realizing how terrible sin is should make us want the Holy Spirit’s help. We need His help to control the bad thoughts and emotions inside us.

7.o: Christ never put any from him that fell at his feet, which a poor trembling soul may do. As she was a good woman, so a good mother. This sent her to Christ. His saying, Let the children first be filled, shows that there was mercy for the Gentiles, and not far off. She spoke, not as making light of the mercy, but magnifying the abundance of miraculous cures among the Jews, in comparison with which a single cure was but as a crumb. Thus, while proud Pharisees are left by the blessed Saviour, he manifests his compassion to poor humbled sinners, who look to him for children's bread. He still goes about to seek and save the lost.
7.m: Christ never turned anyone away who knelt before him. A humble, worried person could do this. Because she was a good woman and a good mother, she went to Christ. When Jesus said, “Let the children be fed first,” it showed that help would come to the Gentiles too, though not right away. She didn’t speak as if she thought little of the mercy, but rather emphasized how many miracles had happened among the Jews compared to just one for her. So while proud Pharisees were often ignored, the Savior showed kindness to humble sinners who looked to him for help. He continues to search out and save those who are lost.

7.o: Here is a cure of one that was deaf and dumb. Those who brought this poor man to Christ, besought him to observe the case, and put forth his power. Our Lord used more outward actions in the doing of this cure than usual. These were only signs of Christ's power to cure the man, to encourage his faith, and theirs that brought him. Though we find great variety in the cases and manner of relief of those who applied to Christ, yet all obtained the relief they sought. Thus it still is in the great concerns of our souls.
7.m:
Here is a story about Jesus healing someone who couldn't hear or speak. The people who brought this person to Jesus asked him to help them. 

Jesus did something different for this healing. He used some gestures to show he had the power to heal. This helped build their faith. 

When people came to Jesus asking for help, he would often do things differently each time. But everyone got what they needed. 

This shows that even today, God helps us in many ways when we need it most.

8.o: Our Lord Jesus encouraged the meanest to come to him for life and grace. Christ knows and considers our frames. The bounty of Christ is always ready; to show that, he repeated this miracle. His favours are renewed, as our wants and necessities are. And those need not fear want, who have Christ to live upon by faith, and do so with thanksgiving.
8.m:
Jesus encourages everyone, no matter how humble, to come to him for life and grace. He understands our needs. 

Christ’s generosity is always available. To prove this, he performed the same miracle again. Just like our needs keep coming up, his help keeps returning.

Those who trust in Christ for their daily needs and give thanks will never go without.

8.o: Obstinate unbelief will have something to say, though ever so unreasonable. Christ refused to answer their demand. If they will not be convinced, they shall not. Alas! what cause we have to lament for those around us, who destroy themselves and others by their perverse and obstinate unbelief, and enmity to the gospel! When we forget the works of God, and distrust him, we should chide ourselves severely, as Christ here reproves his disciples. How is it that we so often mistake his meaning, disregard his warnings, and distrust his providence?
8.m:
Unbelieving people will come up with excuses, even if they don't make sense. Jesus refused to answer them because if they won’t believe, there’s nothing he can do. We should feel sad for those around us who hurt themselves and others by refusing to believe the good news. 

When we ignore God’s work and doubt him, we should be very hard on ourselves, just like Jesus was hard on his followers. Why do we misunderstand him so much? Why do we ignore his warnings and doubt his care for us?

8.o: Here is a blind man brought to Christ by his friends. Therein appeared the faith of those that brought him. If those who are spiritually blind, do not pray for themselves, yet their friends and relations should pray for them, that Christ would be pleased to touch them. The cure was wrought gradually, which was not usual in our Lord's miracles. Christ showed in what method those commonly are healed by his grace, who by nature are spiritually blind. At first, their knowledge is confused; but, like the light of the morning, it shines more and more to the perfect day, and then they see all things clearly. Slighting Christ's favours is forfeiting them; and he will make those who do so know the worth of privileges by the want of them.
8.m:
A blind man was brought to Jesus by his friends. This shows the faith of those who brought him. Even if someone can't ask for help themselves, their family and friends should pray for them so that Jesus might heal them.

The healing didn’t happen right away, which wasn’t normal for Jesus’ miracles. He showed how people usually get better spiritually. At first, their understanding is unclear, like early morning light. But over time, it gets brighter until everything becomes clear.

If we don’t value the gifts Jesus gives us, we might lose them. Then we’ll realize how much we need them when they’re gone.

8.o: These things are written, that we may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God. These miracles of our Lord assure us that he was not conquered, but a Conqueror. Now the disciples are convinced that Jesus is the Christ; they may bear to hear of his sufferings, of which Christ here begins to give them notice. He sees that amiss in what we say and do, of which we ourselves are not aware, and knows what manner of spirit we are of, when we ourselves do not. The wisdom of man is folly, when it pretends to limit the Divine counsels. Peter did not rightly understand the nature of Christ's kingdom.
8.m: These things are written so we can believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God. His miracles show us that he was a conqueror, not someone who was defeated. Now the disciples know that Jesus is the Christ, so they can handle hearing about his suffering, which Jesus starts telling them about. He knows all the wrong things we do, even the ones we don't realize. Human wisdom often fails when trying to understand God’s plans. Peter didn’t fully grasp the kind of kingdom Christ would have.

8.o: Frequent notice is taken of the great flocking there was to Christ for help in various cases. All are concerned to know this, if they expect him to heal their souls. They must not indulge the ease of the body. As the happiness of heaven with Christ, is enough to make up for the loss of life itself for him, so the gain of all the world in sin, will not make up for the ruin of the soul by sin. And there is a day coming, when the cause of Christ will appear as glorious, as some now think it mean and contemptible. May we think of that season, and view every earthly object as we shall do at that great day.
8.m:
The Bible often talks about people going to Jesus for help with different problems. Everyone should care about this because they need Jesus to heal them too. People shouldn’t get lazy or comfortable. 

Just like how being happy with Jesus in heaven makes up for losing anything on Earth, having everything in the world through sin won’t fix the damage sin causes to the soul. 

There will be a time when everyone sees how wonderful Jesus’ cause really is, even though some might think it’s small and unimportant now. We should remember that day and see all things on Earth as we will at the end of days.

9.o: Here is a prediction of the near approach Christ's kingdom. A glimpse of that kingdom was given in the transfiguration of Christ. It is good to be away from the world, and alone with Christ: and how good to be with Christ glorified in heaven with all the saints! But when it is well with us, we are apt not to care for others, and in the fulness of our enjoyments, we forget the many wants of our brethren. God owns Jesus, and accepts him as his beloved Son, and is ready to accept us in him. Therefore we must own and accept him as our beloved Saviour, and must give up ourselves to be ruled by him. Christ does not leave the soul, when joys and comforts leave it. Jesus explained to the disciples the prophecy about Elias. This was very suitable to the ill usage of John Baptist.
9.m:
Here is a prediction of Christ’s kingdom coming soon. A preview of that kingdom appeared during the time when Jesus changed appearance on the mountain. Being alone with Christ is wonderful, especially in heaven with all the saints. But when things are going well for us, we often don’t think about others. We might ignore their needs while enjoying our blessings.

God recognizes Jesus as his beloved Son and accepts him. So we should see Jesus as our beloved Savior and follow him. When Jesus leaves, he doesn't abandon people even if joy and comfort do. 

Jesus explained the prophecy about Elijah to the disciples. This fit well with how badly John the Baptist was treated.

9.o: The father of the suffering youth reflected on the want of power in the disciples; but Christ will have him reckon the disappointment to the want of faith. Very much is promised to our believing. If thou canst believe, it is possible that thy hard heart may be softened, thy spiritual diseases may be cured; and, weak as thou art, thou mayest be able to hold out to the end. Those that complain of unbelief, must look up to Christ for grace to help them against it, and his grace will be sufficient for them. Whom Christ cures, he cures effectually. But Satan is unwilling to be driven from those that have been long his slaves, and, when he cannot deceive or destroy the sinner, he will cause him all the terror that he can. The disciples must not think to do their work always with the same ease; some services call for more than ordinary pains.
9.m: The father of the sick young man thought the disciples lacked the power to heal. But Jesus said it was really because of a lack of faith. Faith can accomplish many things. If you can believe, even a stubborn heart might soften, and your spiritual problems could be healed. Even if you’re weak, you can still stay strong until the end. People who struggle with doubt should ask Christ for help to overcome it, and he’ll give them enough grace to do so. When Christ heals someone, he heals them completely. But Satan doesn’t want to leave people he’s had control over for a long time. So when he can't trick or hurt them anymore, he tries to scare them. The disciples shouldn’t expect everything to be easy. Some tasks require extra effort.

9.o: The time of Christ's suffering drew nigh. Had he been delivered into the hands of devils, and they had done this, it had not been so strange; but that men should thus shamefully treat the Son of man, who came to redeem and save them, is wonderful. Still observe that when Christ spake of his death, he always spake of his resurrection, which took the reproach of it from himself, and should have taken the grief of it from his disciples. Many remain ignorant because they are ashamed to inquire. Alas! that while the Saviour teaches so plainly the things which belong to his love and grace, men are so blinded that they understand not his sayings. We shall be called to account about our discourses, and to account for our disputes, especially about being greater than others. Those who are most humble and self-denying, most resemble Christ, and shall be most tenderly owned by him. This Jesus taught them by a sign; whoever shall receive one like this child, receives me. Many have been like the disciples, ready to silence men who have success in preaching to sinners repentance in Christ's name, because they follow not with them. Our Lord blamed the apostles, reminding them that he who wrought miracles in his name would not be likely to hurt his cause. If sinners are brought to repent, to believe in the Saviour, and to live sober, righteous, and godly lives, we then see that the Lord works by the preacher.
9.m:
Christ's time to suffer was coming soon. It wasn't surprising if evil spirits had caused this, but it was shocking that people were treating the Son of Man so badly, even though He came to save them. Notice that whenever Jesus talked about dying, He also spoke about rising again. This took away the shame of His death and should have comforted His followers.

Many people stay ignorant because they're too embarrassed to ask questions. Sadly, even though Jesus clearly taught about His love and grace, many don’t understand what He said. 

We will be judged based on our words and arguments, especially if those arguments try to show someone as better than another. The more humble and self-sacrificing you are, the more you look like Christ, and He will honor you for it.

Jesus taught this lesson using an example. Whoever welcomes a person like this little child, welcomes Me. Some people act like the disciples did, trying to quiet down preachers who tell sinners to turn back to God through Jesus’ name, just because these preachers aren’t following along with them. 

Our Lord corrected the apostles, telling them that anyone working miracles in His name wouldn’t harm His work. When sinners start to feel sorry, believe in the Savior, and live good, honest lives, we know the Lord is at work through the preacher.

9.o: It is repeatedly said of the wicked, Their worm dieth not, as well as, The fire is never quenched. Doubtless, remorse of conscience and keen self-reflection are this never-dying worm. Surely it is beyond compare better to undergo all possible pain, hardship, and self-denial here, and to be happy for ever hereafter, than to enjoy all kinds of worldly pleasure for a season, and to be miserable for ever. Like the sacrifices, we must be salted with salt; our corrupt affections must be subdued and mortified by the Holy Spirit. Those that have the salt of grace, must show they have a living principle of grace in their hearts, which works out corrupt dispositions in the soul that would offend God, or our own consciences.
9.m:
The wicked are often described as having a worm that never dies, along with a fire that never goes out. This worm likely represents the guilt and regret they feel forever. It’s much better to suffer now and be happy forever after, rather than to enjoy pleasures briefly and then suffer forever.

Just like sacrifices needed to be seasoned with salt, our sinful desires need to be controlled by the Holy Spirit. People who have God’s grace should let it work within them to get rid of anything that might upset God or their own consciences.

10.o: Wherever Jesus was, the people flocked after him in crowds, and he taught them. Preaching was Christ's constant practice. He here shows that the reason why Moses' law allowed divorce, was such that they ought not to use the permission; it was only for the hardness of their hearts. God himself joined man and wife together; he has fitted them to be comforts and helps for each other. The bond which God has tied, is not to be lightly untied. Let those who are for putting away their wives consider what would become of themselves, if God should deal with them in like manner.
10.m: People always followed Jesus wherever he went, and he taught them. Teaching others was something Jesus did all the time. Here, he explains why Moses allowed divorce—it was because people were so stubborn. But really, they shouldn't have used that excuse. God brought husband and wife together, fitting them perfectly to support and comfort each other. The connection God made isn’t meant to be broken easily. Those thinking about leaving their spouse should think about how they’d feel if God treated them the same way.

10.o: Some parents or nurses brought little children to Christ, that he should touch them, in token of his blessing them. It does not appear that they needed bodily cures, nor were they capable of being taught: but those who had the care of them believed that Christ's blessing would do their souls good; therefore they brought them to him. Jesus ordered that they should be brought to him, and that nothing should be said or done to hinder it. Children should be directed to the Saviour as soon as they are able to understand his words. Also, we must receive the kingdom of God as little children; we must stand affected to Christ and his grace, as little children to their parents, nurses, and teachers.
10.m:
Some parents and caregivers brought small children to Jesus so He could bless them. The kids didn't need physical healing or teaching. But those looking after them thought Jesus' blessing would help their spirits grow. So they took the children to Him.

Jesus told people to bring the children to Him. Nothing should stop them from coming. We should introduce children to the Savior as soon as they can understand His message. 

Also, we need to accept God’s kingdom like little children. We should trust in Christ and His grace just as much as children trust their parents, caregivers, and teachers.

10.o: This young ruler showed great earnestness. He asked what he should do now, that he might be happy for ever. Most ask for good to be had in this world; any good, Ps 4:6; he asks for good to be done in this world, in order to enjoy the greatest good in the other world. Christ encouraged this address by assisting his faith, and by directing his practice. But here is a sorrowful parting between Jesus and this young man. He asks Christ what he shall do more than he has done, to obtain eternal life; and Christ puts it to him, whether he has indeed that firm belief of, and that high value for eternal life which he seems to have. Is he willing to bear a present cross, in expectation of future crown? The young man was sorry he could not be a follower of Christ upon easier terms; that he could not lay hold on eternal life, and keep hold of his worldly possessions too. He went away grieved. See Mt 6:24, Ye cannot serve God and mammon.
10.m:
This young ruler showed real concern. He wanted to know what he needed to do to be happy forever. Most people just want good things in this world, but he wanted to do good things now so he could get the best reward in the next world. 

Jesus helped him by strengthening his faith and guiding his actions. But there was sadness at their parting. The young man asked Jesus what else he could do besides what he already did to gain eternal life. Jesus questioned if he truly believed in and valued eternal life enough. Was he ready to face current challenges for future rewards?

The young man felt upset because he couldn't follow Jesus without giving up some of his wealth. He left feeling sad. As Jesus said, you can’t serve both God and money (Matthew 6:24).

10.o: Christ took this occasion to speak to his disciples about the difficulty of the salvation of those who have abundance of this world. Those who thus eagerly seek the wealth of the world, will never rightly prize Christ and his grace. Also, as to the greatness of the salvation of those who have but little of this world, and leave it for Christ. The greatest trial of a good man's constancy is, when love to Jesus calls him to give up love to friends and relatives. Even when gainers by Christ, let them still expect to suffer for him, till they reach heaven. Let us learn contentment in a low state, and to watch against the love of riches in a high one. Let us pray to be enabled to part with all, if required, in Christ's service, and to use all we are allowed to keep in his service.
10.m:
Jesus used this chance to talk to his followers about how hard it is for rich people to get saved. People who really want money won't value Christ and his help much. 

Also, he talked about how amazing it is when poor people choose to follow Christ instead. A true test of someone’s faith happens when loving Jesus means giving up things from family and friends.

Even after getting benefits from following Christ, people should still be ready to face trouble for him until they go to heaven. We should be happy with what we have, even if it's little. And if we have more, we shouldn’t love money too much.

We need to be ready to give everything up if needed for Christ’s sake. And we should use whatever we have to serve him.

10.o: Christ's going on with his undertaking for the salvation of mankind, was, is, and will be, the wonder of all his disciples. Worldly honour is a glittering thing, with which the eyes of Christ's own disciples have many times been dazzled. Our care must be, that we may have wisdom and grace to know how to suffer with him; and we may trust him to provide what the degrees of our glory shall be. Christ shows them that dominion was generally abused in the world. If Jesus would gratify all our desires, it would soon appear that we desire fame or authority, and are unwilling to taste of his cup, or to have his baptism; and should often be ruined by having our prayers answered. But he loves us, and will only give his people what is good for them.
10.m:
Jesus' work to save people has always amazed his followers. 

Worldly honor can blind even his closest friends. We need to stay wise and faithful so we can suffer with him. Trust him to decide our rewards.

Jesus told them that leaders often misuse their power. If he gave everyone what they wanted, it would show they cared more about fame than suffering with him. They might get things that aren't actually good for them.

But Jesus knows best and only gives his followers what is truly good.

10.o: Bartimeus had heard of Jesus and his miracles, and learning that he was passing by, hoped to recover his eyesight. In coming to Christ for help and healing, we should look to him as the promised Messiah. The gracious calls Christ gives us to come to him, encourage our hope, that if we come to him we shall have what we come for. Those who would come to Jesus, must cast away the garment of their own sufficiency, must free themselves from every weight, and the sin that, like long garments, most easily besets them, Heb 12:1. He begged that his eyes might be opened. It is very desirable to be able to earn our bread; and where God has given men limbs and senses, it is a shame, by foolishness and slothfulness, to make themselves, in effect, blind and lame. His eyes were opened. Thy faith has made thee whole: faith in Christ as the Son of David, and in his pity and power; not thy repeated words, but thy faith; Christ setting thy faith to work. Let sinners be exhorted to imitate blind Bartimeus. Where the gospel is preached, or the written words of truth circulated, Jesus is passing by, and this is the opportunity. It is not enough to come to Christ for spiritual healing, but, when we are healed, we must continue to follow him; that we may honour him, and receive instruction from him. Those who have spiritual eyesight, see that beauty in Christ which will draw them to run after him.
10.m:
Bartimeus had heard about Jesus and his miracles. When he learned that Jesus was nearby, he hoped to get his sight back. To ask Jesus for help, we should think of him as the promised savior. 

Jesus invites people to come to him, and this makes us hopeful that we’ll get what we need. People who want to go to Jesus must leave behind anything that holds them back, just like taking off heavy clothes. 

He asked Jesus to heal his blindness. Being able to see again would let him earn money. But it’s shameful to waste gifts from God by being lazy or foolish. 

His eyes were healed. You have faith, so now you’re well. Faith in Jesus as the son of David and trust in his kindness and power helped him. Not because he kept asking, but because he believed. 

Sinners should copy Bartimeus. When the good news is shared, Jesus passes by, offering an opening to seek him out. Just going to Jesus isn’t enough—we must keep following him afterward too. This honors him and lets us learn more from him. 

Those who can see spiritually will notice Jesus’ beauty and want to stay close to him.

11.o: Christ's coming into Jerusalem thus remarkably, shows that he was not afraid of the power and malice of his enemies. This would encourage his disciples who were full of fear. Also, that he was not disquieted at the thoughts of his approaching sufferings. But all marked his humiliation; and these matters teach us not to mind high things, but to condescend to those of low estate. How ill it becomes Christians to take state, when Christ was so far from claiming it! They welcomed his person; Blessed is he that cometh, the “He that should come,” so often promised, so long expected; he comes in the name of the Lord. Let him have our best affections; he is a blessed Saviour, and brings blessings to us, and blessed be He that sent him. Praises be to our God, who is in the highest heavens, over all, God blessed for ever.
11.m:
When Jesus entered Jerusalem in such an obvious way, it showed that he wasn’t scared of his enemies. This helped encourage his followers who were very worried. It also meant that he wasn’t upset about the suffering ahead of him. All of this highlighted his humility. 

These events teach us not to think too highly of ourselves but to treat people who are less fortunate with respect. It’s wrong for Christians to act important when Jesus did not claim any special status for himself.

People greeted Jesus warmly. They said, "Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord." This refers to the one who was promised and awaited for so long. He brings salvation and blessings, and we should welcome him with our best wishes. We should praise God, who is in heaven above all, forever blessed.

11.o: Christ looked to find some fruit, for the time of gathering figs, though it was near, was not yet come; but he found none. He made this fig-tree an example, not to the trees, but to the men of that generation. It was a figure of the doom upon the Jewish church, to which he came seeking fruit, but found none. Christ went to the temple, and began to reform the abuses in its courts, to show that when the Redeemer came to Zion, it was to turn away ungodliness from Jacob. The scribes and the chief priests sought, not how they might make their peace with him, but how they might destroy him. A desperate attempt, which they could not but fear was fighting against God.
11.m:
Jesus wanted to find some fruit on the tree, even though the time for picking figs wasn't quite here yet. But there were no figs at all. Jesus used this fig tree as an example, not just for the trees, but for the people of his time. This tree showed what would happen to the Jewish church because Jesus came looking for good things but found nothing.

Then Jesus went to the temple and started fixing problems there. He did this to show that when the Savior comes to Jerusalem, he will get rid of bad behavior among the people.

The teachers and leaders didn’t try to become friends with Jesus. Instead, they tried to kill him. This was a dangerous plan, and they had to be afraid that they were going up against God.

11.o: The disciples could not think why that fig-tree should so soon wither away; but all wither who reject Christ; it represented the state of the Jewish church. We should rest in no religion that does not make us fruitful in good works. Christ taught them from hence to pray in faith. It may be applied to that mighty faith with which all true Christians are endued, and which does wonders in spiritual things. It justifies us, and so removes mountains of guilt, never to rise up in judgment against us. It purifies the heart, and so removes mountains of corruption, and makes them plain before the grace of God. One great errand to the throne of grace is to pray for the pardon of our sins; and care about this ought to be our daily concern.
11.m:
The disciples couldn't figure out why the fig tree dried up so quickly. But anyone who rejects Christ will dry up too. The fig tree showed what would happen to the Jewish church.

We shouldn’t settle for any religion that doesn’t help us do good deeds. 

Christ used this to teach his followers to pray with real faith. This strong faith helps true believers do amazing things spiritually. Faith forgives us, taking away our guilt forever. It cleanses our hearts, removing all bad habits and making us pure before God.

One main reason we go to God in prayer is to ask for forgiveness of our sins. Caring about this should be part of our daily lives.

11.o: Our Saviour shows how near akin his doctrine and baptism were to those of John; they had the same design and tendency, to bring in the gospel kingdom. These elders did not deserve to be taught; for it was plain that they contended not for truth, but victory: nor did he need to tell them; for the works he did, told them plainly he had authority from God; since no man could do the miracles which he did, unless God were with him.
11.m:
Our Savior showed how closely related his teachings and baptism were to John’s. They both aimed to bring in the kingdom of heaven. 

These leaders didn’t deserve to be taught because they cared more about winning arguments than telling the truth. And Jesus didn’t need to explain himself either, because his actions proved he had God’s approval. No one could perform such miracles unless God was with them.

12.o: Christ showed in parables, that he would lay aside the Jewish church. It is sad to think what base usage God's faithful ministers have met with in all ages, from those who have enjoyed the privileges of the church, but have not brought forth fruit answerable. God at length sent his Son, his Well-beloved; and it might be expected that he whom their Master loved, they also should respect and love; but instead of honouring him because he was the Son and Heir, they therefore hated him. But the exaltation of Christ was the Lord's doing; and it is his doing to exalt him in our hearts, and to set up his throne there; and if this be done, it cannot but be marvellous in our eyes. The Scriptures, and faithful preachers, and the coming of Christ in the flesh, call on us to render due praise to God in our lives. Let sinners beware of a proud, carnal spirit; if they revile or despise the preachers of Christ, they would have done so their Master, had they lived when he was upon earth.
12.m:
Jesus taught using parables to show that he would replace the Jewish church. It’s sad to see how badly God’s faithful ministers have been treated by people who enjoy church privileges but don’t produce good results.

God sent His Son, His Beloved One. People were supposed to honor Him because He was God’s Son and heir. Instead, they hated Him even more because He was God’s Son and heir.

But God raised Jesus up high. And God raises Jesus up in our hearts too. When He does that, we’re amazed.

The Bible, faithful teachers, and Jesus himself in person call us to give God proper praise through our actions.

Sinners need to watch out for pride and selfishness. If they insult or look down on the people preaching about Christ, they would have done the same to Jesus if He were here now.

12.o: The enemies of Christ would be thought desirous to know their duty, when really they hoped that which soever side he took of the question, they might find occasion to accuse him. Nothing is more likely to insnare the followers of Christ, than bringing them to meddle with disputes about worldly politics. Jesus avoided the snare, by referring to the submission they had already made as a nation; and all that heard him, marvelled at the great wisdom of his answer. Many will praise the words of a sermon, who will not be commanded by the doctrines of it.
12.m:
The enemies of Christ pretended to want to know what was right, hoping instead to find a way to accuse him. It’s very likely to trap those who follow Christ by getting them involved in debates about political matters. 

Jesus avoided the trap by pointing out their country’s past actions. Everyone who heard him marveled at how wisely he answered.

Many people will praise the words of a sermon without actually following its teachings.

12.o: A right knowledge of the Scripture, as the fountain whence all revealed religion now flows, and the foundation on which it is built, is the best preservative against error. Christ put aside the objection of the Sadducees, who were the scoffing infidels of that day, by setting the doctrine of the future state in a true light. The relation between husband and wife, though appointed in the earthly paradise, will not be known in the heavenly one. It is no wonder if we confuse ourselves with foolish errors, when we form our ideas of the world of spirits by the affairs of this world of sense. It is absurd to think that the living God should be the portion and happiness of a man if he is for ever dead; and therefore it is certain that Abraham's soul exists and acts, though now for a time separate from the body. Those that deny the resurrection greatly err, and ought to be told so. Let us seek to pass through this dying world, with a joyful hope of eternal happiness, and of a glorious resurrection.
12.m: Knowing the Bible well, since it’s where all religious teachings come from today, helps protect us from mistakes. Jesus answered the Sadducees, who were the doubters back then, by explaining what life after death really means. In heaven, there won’t be marriages like here on earth. We get confused about spiritual things because we try to compare them to physical things around us. It doesn’t make sense to say someone gets joy from God forever if they’re actually dead. So, Abraham’s spirit must still exist even without his body. People who don’t believe in coming back to life after death are wrong. We should tell them that. As we live in this temporary world, let’s have hope for eternal joy and a wonderful new life someday.

12.o: Those who sincerely desire to be taught their duty, Christ will guide in judgment, and teach his way. He tells the scribe that the great commandment, which indeed includes all, is, that of loving God with all our hearts.
