Wherever this is the ruling principle in the soul, there is a disposition to every other duty. Loving God with all our heart, will engage us to every thing by which he will be pleased. The sacrifices only represented the atonements for men's transgressions of the moral law; they were of no power except as they expressed repentance and faith in the promised Saviour, and as they led to moral obedience. And because we have not thus loved God and man, but the very reverse, therefore we are condemned sinners; we need repentance, and we need mercy. Christ approved what the scribe said, and encouraged him. He stood fair for further advance; for this knowledge of the law leads to conviction of sin, to repentance, to discovery of our need of mercy, and understanding the way of justification by Christ.
12.m:
Christ will help those who truly want to learn about doing what’s right. He told the teacher that the most important rule is to love God with all your heart. When you do that, you’ll naturally do everything else that pleases God too.

Loving God fully makes you willing to do whatever brings Him joy. Sacrifices only showed people’s sorrow for breaking God’s rules and their trust in Jesus. They helped if they led to obeying God morally.

Since we haven’t loved God and others as we should, we’re guilty sinners. We need to turn from our sins and ask for forgiveness. 

Jesus agreed with what the teacher said and praised him. This knowledge of God’s laws helps people see their wrongs, feel sorry, realize they need mercy, and understand how Jesus can save them.

12.o: When we attend to what the Scriptures declare, as to the person and offices of Christ, we shall be led to confess him as our Lord and God; to obey him as our exalted Redeemer. If the common people hear these things gladly, while the learned and distinguished oppose, the former are happy, and the latter to be pitied. And as sin, disguised with a show of piety, is double iniquity, so its doom will be doubly heavy.
12.m:
When we pay attention to what the Bible says about Jesus, we will admit he is our Lord and God. We will follow him as our Savior who has been raised up high. 

If regular people listen happily to these teachings, while educated and important people reject them, the ordinary folks are blessed, and those who refuse to accept it deserve pity.

And just as sin covered by a pretense of being holy makes someone twice guilty, their punishment will also be twice as severe.

12.o: Let us not forget that Jesus still sees the treasury. He knows how much, and from what motives, men give to his cause. He looks at the heart, and what our views are, in giving alms; and whether we do it as unto the Lord, or only to be seen of men. It is so rare to find any who would not blame this widow, that we cannot expect to find many who will do like to her; and yet our Saviour commends her, therefore we are sure that she did well and wisely. The feeble efforts of the poor to honour their Saviour, will be commended in that day, when the splendid actions of unbelievers will be exposed to contempt.
12.m:
Don't forget that Jesus still watches the money being put in the treasury. He knows how much people give and why they give it. He looks at our hearts and motives when we give charity. Does our giving come from a desire to please God or just to show off to others? 

It’s unusual to find someone who wouldn’t criticize this widow for giving all she had. But Jesus praises her, which means she did right. Her small gift to honor her Savior will be praised on judgment day, while the big donations from those who don’t believe won’t matter.

13.o: See how little Christ values outward pomp, where there is not real purity of heart. He looks with pity upon the ruin of precious souls, and weeps over them, but we do not find him look with pity upon the ruin of a fine house. Let us then be reminded how needful it is for us to have a more lasting abode in heaven, and to be prepared for it by the influences of the Holy Spirit, sought in the earnest use of all the means of grace.
13.m: See how little Jesus cares about fancy things when people’s hearts aren’t pure. He feels sorry for lost souls and cries for them, but he doesn’t feel bad if someone loses their nice house. This reminds us that we need a home in heaven that will last forever. We should ask the Holy Spirit to help us get ready for it by using everything God gives us to grow closer to Him.

13.o: Our Lord Jesus, in reply to the disciples' question, does not so much satisfy their curiosity as direct their consciences. When many are deceived, we should thereby be awakened to look to ourselves. And the disciples of Christ, if it be not their own fault, may enjoy holy security and peace of mind, when all around is in disorder. But they must take heed that they are not drawn away from Christ and their duty to him, by the sufferings they will meet with for his sake. They shall be hated of all men: trouble enough! Yet the work they were called to should be carried on and prosper. Though they may be crushed and borne down, the gospel cannot be. The salvation promised is more than deliverance from evil, it is everlasting blessedness.
13.m:
Jesus answered his disciples' question not just to satisfy their curiosity, but to guide them morally. 

When many people are confused, we should pay closer attention to our own beliefs. 

If Christians stay faithful, they can feel safe and at peace even when everything else seems chaotic. 

But they need to watch out that they don't get pulled away from following Christ because of the troubles they might face for him. 

They will be hated by everyone, which is very difficult. 

Yet, the work they do for God will continue and succeed. 

Even if they are beaten down, the good news about Jesus will keep spreading. 

The salvation promised goes beyond just being saved from bad things—it means eternal happiness.

13.o: The Jews in rebelling against the Romans, and in persecuting the Christians, hastened their own ruin apace. Here we have a prediction of that ruin which came upon them within less than forty years after this. Such destruction and desolation, that the like cannot be found in any history. Promises of power to persevere, and cautions against falling away, well agree with each other. But the more we consider these things, the more we shall see abundant cause to flee without delay for refuge to Christ, and to renounce every earthly object, for the salvation of our souls.
13.m: The Jews were speeding up their own downfall by rebelling against the Romans and persecuting the Christians. This passage predicts the destruction that would come to them within about forty years. It will be so terrible that nothing like it has ever been recorded in history. The promises to keep us strong in faith and warnings not to turn away from God fit together well. As we think about these things, we see even more reason to quickly run to Christ for safety and leave behind all worldly things for the sake of saving our souls.

13.o: The disciples had confounded the destruction of Jerusalem and the end of the world. This mistake Christ set right, and showed that the day of Christ's coming, and the day of judgment, shall be after that tribulation. Here he foretells the final dissolution of the present frame and fabric of the world. Also, the visible appearance of the Lord Jesus coming in the clouds, and the gathering together of all the elect to him.
13.m: The disciples were confused about when Jerusalem would be destroyed and when the world would end. Jesus corrected them and explained that his return and the final judgment will happen after the great trouble. He also told them that one day the current order of things will come to an end. Then, Jesus will visibly appear in the sky, and all chosen people will be gathered to him.

13.o: We have the application of this prophetic sermon. As to the destruction of Jerusalem, expect it to come very shortly. As to the end of the world, do not inquire when it will come, for of that day and that hour knoweth no man. Christ, as God, could not be ignorant of anything; but the Divine wisdom which dwelt in our Saviour, communicated itself to his human soul according to the Divine pleasure. As to both, our duty is to watch and pray. Our Lord Jesus, when he ascended on high, left something for all his servants to do. We ought to be always upon our watch, in expectation of his return. This applies to Christ's coming to us at our death, as well as to the general judgment. We know not whether our Master will come in the days of youth, or middle age, or old age; but, as soon as we are born, we begin to die, and therefore we must expect death. Our great care must be, that, whenever our Lord comes, he may not find us secure, indulging in ease and sloth, mindless of our work and duty. He says to all, Watch, that you may be found in peace, without spot, and blameless.
13.m:
We see the practical message from this prophecy. For the destruction of Jerusalem, expect it to happen very soon. As for the end of the world, don’t ask when it will come because no one knows the exact time. Even though Jesus knew everything as God, he chose to share only what was necessary with his human nature. 

For both events, our job is to stay alert and pray. When Jesus went up to heaven, he gave his followers tasks to complete. We should always be ready, waiting for him to return. This applies to his coming back at the end of the world as well as when someone dies.

We don't know if our master will come during our youth, middle years, or later life. But from the moment we're born, we start dying bit by bit. So we need to be prepared for death at any time. Our main concern must be that when Jesus returns, he doesn’t find us lazy and neglecting our duties. He tells everyone to stay alert so that when he comes, we’ll be peaceful, pure, and free from fault.

14.o: Did Christ pour out his soul unto death for us, and shall we think any thing too precious for him? Do we give him the precious ointment of our best affections? Let us love him with all the heart, though it is common for zeal and affection to be misunderstood and blamed; and remember that charity to the poor will not excuse any from particular acts of piety to the Lord Jesus. Christ commended this woman's pious attention to the notice of believers in all ages. Those who honour Christ he will honour. Covetousness was Judas' master lust, and that betrayed him to the sin of betraying his Master; the devil suited his temptation to that, and so conquered him. And see what wicked contrivances many have in their sinful pursuits; but what appears to forward their plans, will prove curses in the end.
14.m:
Did Christ give up his life for us, and should anything seem too valuable for him? Do we offer him our finest feelings and thoughts? We should love him with all our hearts, even if others misunderstand or criticize us. Giving to the poor won't replace showing special devotion to Jesus. 

Christ praised this woman’s loving act for people to remember forever. Those who honor Christ, He will honor. 

Judas was greedy, which led him to betray Christ. The devil used Judas’ greed to trick him. Many people come up with clever schemes for their sins, but these plans often backfire and become curses.

14.o: Nothing could be less the result of human foresight than the events here related. But our Lord knows all things about us before they come to pass. If we admit him, he will dwell in our hearts. The Son of man goes, as it is written of him, as a lamb to the slaughter; but woe to that man by whom he is betrayed! God's permitting the sins of men, and bringing glory to himself out of them, does not oblige them to sin; nor will this be any excuse for their guilt, or lessen their punishment.
14.m:
Nothing could be more unexpected than what happens here. But Jesus knows everything about us before it even happens. If we let him, he will live in our hearts. The Son of Man goes, just like it was written, like a lamb going to be killed. But trouble will come to the person who betrays him!

When God lets people do wrong things and uses those mistakes to show his greatness, it doesn't mean people should go ahead and sin. This won’t excuse anyone’s guilt or reduce their punishment either.

14.o: The Lord's supper is food for the soul, therefore a very little of that which is for the body, as much as will serve for a sign, is enough. It was instituted by the example and the practice of our Master, to remain in force till his second coming. It was instituted with blessing and giving of thanks, to be a memorial of Christ's death. Frequent mention is made of his precious blood, as the price of our redemption. How comfortable is this to poor repenting sinners, that the blood of Christ is shed for many! If for many, why not for me? It was a sign of the conveyance of the benefits purchased for us by his death. Apply the doctrine of Christ crucified to yourselves; let it be meat and drink to your souls, strengthening and refreshing your spiritual life. It was to be an earnest and foretaste of the happiness of heaven, and thereby to put us out of taste for the pleasures and delights of sense. Every one that has tasted spiritual delights, straightway desires eternal ones. Though the great Shepherd passed through his sufferings without one false step, yet his followers often have been scattered by the small measure of sufferings allotted to them. How very apt we are to think well of ourselves, and to trust our own hearts! It was ill done of Peter thus to answer his Master, and not with fear and trembling. Lord, give me grace to keep me from denying thee.
14.m:
The Lord's Supper is food for the soul, so just a small amount of bread and wine, enough to represent it, is all that’s needed. Our Master set up this meal by his example and continued practice until he returns again. He blessed and gave thanks over these items to remember his death. 

We hear a lot about Jesus' blood because it paid for our sins. This is good news for people who feel sorry for their wrongs—Jesus died for many, so if it’s for many, why not for me?

It shows the blessings won by his sacrifice. Take what Jesus taught seriously and let it strengthen you spiritually. The meal also gives a hint of the joy waiting in heaven, making earthly pleasures less appealing.

Anyone who tastes true spiritual joys wants more of those forever. Even though Jesus stayed strong during his suffering, his followers sometimes run away at even small troubles. We’re quick to think highly of ourselves and trust our own feelings too much.

Peter did wrong by answering Jesus like that instead of being careful. Lord, help me stay loyal to you.

14.o: Christ's sufferings began with the sorest of all, those in his soul. He began to be sorely amazed; words not used in St. Matthew, but very full of meaning. The terrors of God set themselves in array against him, and he allowed him to contemplate them. Never was sorrow like unto his at this time. Now he was made a curse for us; the curses of the law were laid upon him as our Surety. He now tasted death, in all the bitterness of it. This was that fear of which the apostle speaks, the natural fear of pain and death, at which human nature startles. Can we ever entertain favourable, or even slight thoughts of sin, when we see the painful sufferings which sin, though but reckoned to him, brought on the Lord Jesus? Shall that sit light upon our souls, which sat so heavy upon his? Was Christ in such agony for our sins, and shall we never be in agony about them? How should we look upon Him whom we have pierced, and mourn! It becomes us to be exceedingly sorrowful for sin, because He was so, and never to mock at it. Christ, as Man, pleaded, that, if it were possible, his sufferings might pass from him. As Mediator, he submitted to the will of God, saying, Nevertheless, not what I will, but what thou wilt; I bid it welcome. See how the sinful weakness of Christ's disciples returns, and overpowers them. What heavy clogs these bodies of ours are to our souls! But when we see trouble at the door, we should get ready for it. Alas, even believers often look at the Redeemer's sufferings in a drowsy manner, and instead of being ready to die with Christ, they are not even prepared to watch with him one hour.
14.m:
Christ's suffering started with the worst part—his inner torment. He became deeply troubled. Words not found in Saint Matthew, yet rich in meaning. The fears of God surrounded him, and he faced them fully. His sadness was unlike anyone else’s. At this moment, he took our place by becoming a curse. All the punishments of the law fell on him. He tasted death in its fullest bitterness. This was the fear the apostle talked about—the normal fear of pain and death that makes people afraid. 

Can we think lightly of sin after seeing how much it hurt Jesus? Should we take lightly what caused him such great pain? Did Christ feel such deep anguish over our sins, and shouldn’t we feel anguish too? We should mourn deeply for sin because Jesus did. 

As a man, Christ asked if it could be avoided. Yet as our mediator, he accepted God’s will, saying, “Not my will, but yours.” He welcomed whatever came. Even the followers of Christ showed their weak faith again, falling asleep during his greatest need. Our physical bodies sometimes hold back our spirits. When trouble comes, we should prepare ourselves. Sadly, many believers doze off when thinking about Christ’s suffering, unable to stay awake even for an hour.

14.o: Because Christ appeared not as a temporal prince, but preached repentance, reformation, and a holy life, and directed men's thoughts, and affections, and aims to another world, therefore the Jewish rulers sought to destroy him. Peter wounded one of the band. It is easier to fight for Christ than to die for him. But there is a great difference between faulty disciples and hypocrites. The latter rashly and without thought call Christ Master, and express great affection for him, yet betray him to his enemies. Thus they hasten their own destruction.
14.m: Christ did not come as a worldly ruler. Instead, he taught people to turn from sin, live righteously, and focus on heaven. This made the Jewish leaders want to harm him. When soldiers came to arrest Jesus, Peter attacked one of them. It’s easier to fight for Christ than to die for him. There’s a big difference between followers who make mistakes and those who pretend to be believers. Hypocrites boldly claim to love Christ but then hand him over to his enemies. They bring about their own downfall by doing so.

14.o: We have here Christ's condemnation before the great council of the Jews. Peter followed; but the high priest's fire-side was no proper place, nor his servants proper company, for Peter: it was an entrance into temptation. Great diligence was used to procure false witnesses against Jesus, yet their testimony was not equal to the charge of a capital crime, by the utmost stretch of their law. He was asked, Art thou the Son of the Blessed? that is, the Son of God. For the proof of his being the Son of God, he refers to his second coming. In these outrages we have proofs of man's enmity to God, and of God's free and unspeakable love to man.
14.m:
Here we see Jesus being condemned by the Jewish leaders. Peter followed along, but staying at the high priest’s house wasn’t good for him. The people there were not nice company, and it led Peter into trouble. 

They tried hard to find people who would lie about Jesus, but even then, their lies didn't prove he had done anything really bad according to their laws. They asked him if he was the Son of the Blessed, which means the Son of God. To show he was the Son of God, Jesus talked about his return someday.

These events show how much people hate God and how much God loves us.

14.o: Peter's denying Christ began by keeping at a distance from him. Those that are shy of godliness, are far in the way to deny Christ. Those who think it dangerous to be in company with Christ's disciples, because thence they may be drawn in to suffer for him, will find it much more dangerous to be in company with his enemies, because there they may be drawn in to sin against him. When Christ was admired and flocked after, Peter readily owned him; but will own no relation to him now he is deserted and despised. Yet observe, Peter's repentance was very speedy. Let him that thinketh he standeth take heed lest he fall; and let him that has fallen think of these things, and of his own offences, and return to the Lord with weeping and supplication, seeking forgiveness, and to be raised up by the Holy Spirit.
14.m:
Peter started denying Jesus by staying away from him. People who avoid being religious often end up denying Christ. Those who think it’s risky to hang out with Jesus’ followers because they might get hurt for him will find it even riskier to spend time with those who hate Jesus because they might do wrong to him too.

When people were praising Jesus and following him, Peter would admit he knew him. But now that Jesus is alone and disliked, Peter won’t admit anything about knowing him.

However, Peter felt sorry quickly afterward. Anyone who thinks they’re safe should watch out so they don’t fall. And anyone who has already fallen should remember these things and their own mistakes, then turn back to God with tears and prayers asking for forgiveness and help from the Holy Spirit.

15.o: They bound Christ. It is good for us often to remember the bonds of the Lord Jesus, as bound with him who was bound for us. By delivering up the King, they, in effect, delivered up the kingdom of God, which was, therefore, as by their own consent, taken from them, and given to another nation. Christ gave Pilate a direct answer, but would not answer the witnesses, because the things they alleged were known to be false, even Pilate himself was convinced they were so. Pilate thought that he might appeal from the priests to the people, and that they would deliver Jesus out of the priests' hands. But they were more and more urged by the priests, and cried, Crucify him! Crucify him! Let us judge of persons and things by their merits, and the standard of God's word, and not by common report. The thought that no one ever was so shamefully treated, as the only perfectly wise, holy, and excellent Person that ever appeared on earth, leads the serious mind to strong views of man's wickedness and enmity to God. Let us more and more abhor the evil dispositions which marked the conduct of these persecutors.
15.m:
They tied up Jesus. We should often think about the chains that Jesus wore, since he was chained for us. By handing over the king, they handed over the kingdom of God too. So, the kingdom was taken away from them and given to another group, almost like they agreed to it themselves.

Jesus answered Pilate directly, but did not respond to the witnesses because what they said was known to be lies. Even Pilate knew the witnesses were lying.

Pilate thought the crowd would free Jesus from the priests, but instead, the priests pushed the crowd harder. People shouted, "Crucify him! Crucify him!"

We should judge people and situations based on their true worth and God’s word, not just what we hear others say. Thinking about how someone as perfect as Jesus was treated so badly makes us realize how much people can hate God.

Let’s learn to dislike the mean attitudes that led those people to hurt Jesus.

15.o: Christ met death in its greatest terror. It was the death of the vilest malefactors. Thus the cross and the shame are put together. God having been dishonoured by the sin of man, Christ made satisfaction by submitting to the greatest disgrace human nature could be loaded with. It was a cursed death; thus it was branded by the Jewish law, De 21:23. The Roman soldiers mocked our Lord Jesus as a King; thus in the high priest's hall the servants had mocked him as a Prophet and Saviour. Shall a purple or scarlet robe be matter of pride to a Christian, which was matter of reproach and shame to Christ? He wore the crown of thorns which we deserved, that we might wear the crown of glory which he merited. We were by sin liable to everlasting shame and contempt; to deliver us, our Lord Jesus submitted to shame and contempt. He was led forth with the workers of iniquity, though he did no sin. The sufferings of the meek and holy Redeemer, are ever a source of instruction to the believer, of which, in his best hours, he cannot be weary. Did Jesus thus suffer, and shall I, a vile sinner, fret or repine? Shall I indulge anger, or utter reproaches and threats because of troubles and injuries?
15.m:
Christ faced the worst kind of death. It was the death reserved for the most wicked criminals. So the cross and the shame went hand in hand. Because humans dishonored God through their sins, Christ paid for it by enduring the biggest disgrace possible. This was a shameful death, marked as cursed by Jewish laws, as seen in Deuteronomy 21:23. The Roman guards laughed at Jesus, calling him a king. Earlier, people in the high priest’s house had mocked him as a prophet and savior. Should Christians take pride in fancy clothes when Jesus wore them only to mock him?

Jesus wore the crown of thorns that we deserved so we could receive the crown of glory he earned. Our sins left us open to eternal shame and ridicule. To save us, Jesus accepted shame and mockery. Even though he didn’t do anything wrong, Jesus was led out with other criminals. 

The suffering of the gentle and pure Savior teaches believers a lot. In our best moments, we should always remember these lessons. If Jesus endured such pain, how can I complain about my problems if I am a sinful person? When facing trouble or being hurt, shouldn't I stay calm instead of getting angry or saying mean things?

15.o: The place where our Lord Jesus was crucified, was called the place of a scull; it was the common place of execution; for he was in all respects numbered with the transgressors. Whenever we look unto Christ crucified, we must remember what was written over his head; he is a King, and we must give up ourselves to be his subjects, as Israelites indeed. They crucified two thieves with him, and him in the midst; they thereby intended him great dishonour. But it was foretold that he should be numbered with the transgressors, because he was made sin for us. Even those who passed by railed at him. They told him to come down from the cross, and they would believe; but they did not believe, though he gave them a more convincing sign when he came up from the grave. With what earnestness will the man who firmly believes the truth, as made known by the sufferings of Christ, seek for salvation! With what gratitude will he receive the dawning hope of forgiveness and eternal life, as purchased for him by the sufferings and death of the Son of God! and with what godly sorrow will he mourn over the sins which crucified the Lord of glory!
15.m:
The place where Jesus was crucified was called the place of a skull. It was the usual spot for executions. He was treated just like a criminal. 

When we think about Jesus on the cross, we should remember what was written above his head. He is a king, and we need to become his loyal followers.

Two criminals were crucified next to Jesus, one on each side. This was meant to insult him. But it was predicted that he would be counted among sinners because he took on our sins.

Even people passing by mocked him. They said he should come down if he wanted them to believe. But they didn’t really believe, even after he rose from the dead.

Those who truly believe in Jesus’ suffering will eagerly search for salvation. People who trust in what Jesus did will thank God for their hope of forgiveness and eternal life. And they will feel sorry for the sins that led to Jesus' death.

15.o: There was a thick darkness over the land, from noon until three in the afternoon. The Jews were doing their utmost to extinguish the Sun of Righteousness. The darkness signified the cloud which the human soul of Christ was under, when he was making it an offering for sin. He did not complain that his disciples forsook him, but that his Father forsook him. In this especially he was made sin for us. When Paul was to be offered as a sacrifice for the service saints, he could joy and rejoice, Php 2:17; but it is another thing to be offered as a sacrifice for the sin of sinners. At the same instant that Jesus died, the veil of the temple was rent from the top to the bottom. This spake terror to the unbelieving Jews, and was a sign of the destruction of their church and nation. It speaks comfort to all believing Christians, for it signified the laying open a new and living way into the holiest by the blood of Jesus. The confidence with which Christ had openly addressed God as his Father, and committed his soul into his hands, seems greatly to have affected the centurion. Right views of Christ crucified will reconcile the believer to the thought of death; he longs to behold, love, and praise, as he ought, that Saviour who was wounded and pierced to save him from the wrath to come.
15.m:
There was a dark sky from noon until three o'clock in the afternoon. The Jewish people were trying their hardest to put out the light of the Son of righteousness. The darkness showed that Jesus felt very alone as he became an offering for our sins. He didn't complain about his followers leaving him, but rather that God seemed far away. This shows how Jesus took on our sin.

When Paul knew he would die for others, he felt happy, Philippians 2:17. But dying for the sins of everyone else was different. 

At the exact moment Jesus died, the curtain in the temple tore apart from top to bottom. This scared the Jewish people and showed that their religion was ending. But it gave hope to Christian believers because it meant there was now a direct path to God through Jesus' blood.

The soldier saw how Jesus trusted God completely and handed his spirit over to him. Seeing Jesus die helped believers accept their own deaths more calmly. They want to see, love, and praise the one who suffered to save them from God’s anger.

15.o: We are here attending the burial of our Lord Jesus. Oh that we may by grace be planted in the likeness of it! Joseph of Arimathea was one who waited for the kingdom of God. Those who hope for a share in its privileges, must own Christ's cause, when it seems to be crushed. This man God raised up for his service. There was a special providence, that Pilate should be so strict in his inquiry, that there might be no pretence to say Jesus was alive. Pilate gave Joseph leave to take down the body, and do what he pleased with it. Some of the women beheld where Jesus was laid, that they might come after the sabbath to anoint the dead body, because they had not time to do it before. Special notice was taken of Christ's sepulchre, because he was to rise again. And he will not forsake those who trust in him, and call upon him. Death, deprived of its sting, will soon end the believer's sorrows, as it ended those of the Saviour.
15.m:
Here we see the burial of Jesus. May we be like Him through God’s grace!

Joseph of Arimathea believed in the coming kingdom. People hoping for its blessings must support Christ even if He faces trouble. God used Joseph to help bury Jesus.

There was a special reason why Pilate carefully checked if Jesus was really dead. That way, no one could later claim Jesus wasn’t truly dead. 

Pilate allowed Joseph to take Jesus’ body and handle it however he wanted. Some women watched where Jesus was buried so they could return after the Sabbath to anoint His body. They didn't have time to do it earlier.

The place where Jesus was buried got extra attention because He would rise from the dead. God won’t abandon those who put their faith in Him and call on Him. For believers, death loses its power and ends all suffering just as it did for Jesus.

16.o: Nicodemus brought a large quantity of spices, but these good women did not think that enough. The respect others show to Christ, should not hinder us from showing our respect. And those who are carried by holy zeal, to seek Christ diligently, will find the difficulties in their way speedily vanish. When we put ourselves to trouble and expense, from love to Christ, we shall be accepted, though our endeavours are not successful. The sight of the angel might justly have encouraged them, but they were affrighted. Thus many times that which should be matter of comfort to us, through our own mistake, proves a terror to us. He was crucified, but he is glorified. He is risen, he is not here, not dead, but alive again; hereafter you will see him, but you may here see the place where he was laid. Thus seasonable comforts will be sent to those that lament after the Lord Jesus. Peter is particularly named, Tell Peter; it will be most welcome to him, for he is in sorrow for sin. A sight of Christ will be very welcome to a true penitent, and a true penitent is very welcome to a sight of Christ. The men ran with all the haste they could to the disciples; but disquieting fears often hinder us from doing that service to Christ and to the souls of men, which, if faith and the joy of faith were strong, we might do.
16.m:
Nicodemus brought a lot of spices, but the women thought that wasn't enough. We shouldn't let what others do for Christ stop us from showing our respect too. People who really want to find Christ usually overcome any problems in their way. When we work hard and spend money out of love for Christ, God accepts our efforts even if things don’t turn out perfectly.

The women saw an angel, which would normally give them hope, but instead they were scared. Sometimes, things that should comfort us end up scaring us because of our own mistakes.

Jesus was once on the cross, but now he’s honored. He has risen from the dead and isn’t here anymore. You’ll see him later, but right now you can look at the place where his body was laid. Comforts come at the right time for people who miss Jesus.

Peter is mentioned specifically—tell Peter. This news will be especially nice for him since he feels bad about his sins. Seeing Christ makes a real sinner feel better, and Christ welcomes anyone who truly regrets their wrongdoings.

The men rushed quickly to tell the disciples. But often, worries get in the way of serving Christ and helping others. If we had more faith and felt happier, we could serve better.

16.o: Better news cannot be brought to disciples in tears, than to tell them of Christ's resurrection. And we should study to comfort disciples that are mourners, by telling them whatever we have seen of Christ. It was a wise providence that the proofs of Christ's resurrection were given gradually, and admitted cautiously, that the assurance with which the apostles preached this doctrine afterwards might the more satisfy. Yet how slowly do we admit the consolations which the word of God holds forth! Therefore while Christ comforts his people, he often sees it needful to rebuke and correct them for hardness of heart in distrusting his promise, as well as in not obeying his holy precepts.
16.m:
Better news cannot be given to sad followers than to tell them about Jesus rising from the dead. We should try to cheer up those who are crying by sharing what we know about Jesus. 

It was smart that proof of Jesus' resurrection came little by little, so the apostles could believe it fully later on. But how slow we are to accept the comfort the Bible offers us!

So, while Jesus comforts his people, he sometimes needs to gently scold them for not trusting his promises or following his rules.

16.o: The evidences of the truth of the gospel are so full, that those who receive it not, may justly be upbraided with their unbelief. Our blessed Lord renewed his choice of the eleven as his apostles, and commissioned them to go into all the world, to preach his gospel to every creature. Only he that is a true Christian shall be saved through Christ. Simon Magus professed to believe, and was baptized, yet he was declared to be in the bonds of iniquity: see his history in Ac 8:13–25. Doubtless this is a solemn declaration of that true faith which receives Christ in all his characters and offices, and for all the purposes of salvation, and which produces its right effect on the heart and life; not a mere assent, which is a dead faith, and cannot profit. The commission of Christ's ministers extends to every creature throughout the world, and the declarations of the gospel contain not only truths, encouragements, and precepts, but also most awful warnings. Observe what power the apostles should be endued with, for confirming the doctrine they were to preach. These were miracles to confirm the truth of the gospel, and means of spreading the gospel among nations that had not heard it.
16.m:
The proof of the truth of the gospel is so strong that people who don’t accept it can be criticized for not believing.

Our Lord Jesus chose the eleven disciples again and gave them orders to spread his message everywhere to everyone. Only those who truly follow Christ will be saved.

Simon Magus said he believed and got baptized, but he was still called a prisoner of sin. Read about him in Acts 8:13–25. This shows the kind of real faith that accepts Christ fully for our salvation and changes us inside and out. It’s more than just agreeing with ideas—it has to affect how we live.

The job of Jesus’ followers is to share the good news with everyone around the world. Their message includes facts, hope, rules, and serious warnings too.

Notice the special powers given to the apostles to help prove the truth of their teachings. Miracles showed that the gospel was true and helped spread it to places where people hadn’t heard it before.

16.o: After the Lord had spoken he went up into heaven. Sitting is a posture of rest, he had finished his work; and a posture of rule, he took possession of his kingdom. He sat at the right hand of God, which denotes his sovereign dignity and universal power. Whatever God does concerning us, gives to us, or accepts from us, it is by his Son. Now he is glorified with the glory he had before the world. The apostles went forth, and preached every where, far and near. Though the doctrine they preached was spiritual and heavenly, and directly contrary to the spirit and temper of the world; though it met with much opposition, and was wholly destitute of all worldly supports and advantages; yet in a few years the sound went forth unto the ends of the earth. Christ's ministers do not now need to work miracles to prove their message; the Scriptures are proved to be of Divine origin, and this renders those without excuse who reject or neglect them. The effects of the gospel, when faithfully preached, and truly believed, in changing the tempers and characters of mankind, form a constant proof, a miraculous proof, that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation, of all who believe.
16.m:
After Jesus spoke, he went up to heaven. Sitting shows he was done with his work and ready to rule. He sat at God’s right hand, showing his great honor and power over everything. Whenever God acts for us, gives us things, or accepts our offerings, it is through his Son. Now Jesus has the same glory he had before the world began.

The apostles started preaching everywhere, both nearby and far away. Even though what they taught was about spiritual matters and against the ways of the world, and even though they faced lots of opposition and had no worldly help, within just a few years their message spread to distant lands.

Today, Christ’s followers don’t need to perform miracles to show their teachings are true. The Scriptures prove themselves to be from God, making people guilty if they ignore or reject them. When the gospel is preached faithfully and believed sincerely, it changes people’s hearts and lives. This proves that the gospel really is God’s power to save those who believe.
